questions.question:
Let P be a quicksort program to sort numbers in ascending order using the first element as the pivot. Let t_1 and t_2 be the number of comparisons made by P for the inputs {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and {4, 1, 3, 2, 5} respectively. Which one of the following holds?
A. t_1 < t_2
B. t_1 > t_2
C. t_1 = t_2
D. t_1 = t_2 + 1
questions.to_find: Compare the number of comparisons made by first-element-pivot quicksort on a sorted vs unsorted input.

understand.plain: Quicksort uses the first element as the pivot. When the input is already sorted (like {1,2,3,4,5}), picking the first element always creates one partition of size 0 and one of size n-1, which is the worst case and leads to the maximum number of comparisons. For a less-sorted input like {4,1,3,2,5}, the pivot divides the array more evenly, resulting in fewer comparisons overall.

keywords.explain: The element chosen to partition the array; all smaller elements go left, all larger go right.
keywords.example: In {4,1,3,2,5}, choosing 4 as pivot splits into {1,3,2} and {5}.

keywords.explain: Occurs when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element, giving unbalanced partitions of size 0 and n-1 every time.
keywords.example: Sorted array {1,2,3,4,5} with first-element pivot: always picks the minimum, giving O(n^2) comparisons.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='180' viewBox='0 0 520 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='240' height='160' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Input: {1,2,3,4,5}</text><text x='130' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Pivot=1 -> [] | [2,3,4,5]</text><text x='130' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Pivot=2 -> [] | [3,4,5]</text><text x='130' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Pivot=3 -> [] | [4,5]</text><text x='130' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Pivot=4 -> [] | [5]</text><text x='130' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Comparisons: 4+3+2+1 = 10 (Worst)</text><rect x='270' y='10' width='240' height='160' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='390' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Input: {4,1,3,2,5}</text><text x='390' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Pivot=4 -> [1,3,2] | [5]</text><text x='390' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Pivot=1 -> [] | [3,2]</text><text x='390' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Pivot=3 -> [2] | []</text><text x='390' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(done)</text><text x='390' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Comparisons: 4+3+2 = 9 (Better)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two recursion trees showing that sorted input {1,2,3,4,5} with first-element pivot produces completely unbalanced splits (worst case: 10 comparisons), while {4,1,3,2,5} produces more balanced splits with fewer comparisons.

given.aim: Determine whether t_1 (comparisons on sorted input) is greater than, less than, or equal to t_2 (comparisons on partially unsorted input).

terms.meaning: Already sorted in ascending order; first element is always the minimum.
terms.example: Picking pivot 1 from {1,2,3,4,5} leaves left part empty and right part {2,3,4,5}.
terms.connects: Gives worst-case O(n^2) partitioning, maximizing comparisons.

terms.meaning: Unsorted array; first element is 4, which is near the median.
terms.connects: A near-median pivot gives more balanced partitions and fewer total comparisons.

terms.meaning: Total element comparisons made during quicksort on each input respectively.
terms.example: Each partitioning step compares elements against the pivot.
terms.connects: We compare these two totals to determine which input causes more work.

given.plan: Trace the quicksort recursion for both inputs, count comparisons at each level. For {1,2,3,4,5}: worst case, n-1 comparisons at each level => 4+3+2+1 = 10. For {4,1,3,2,5}: more balanced splits => 4+3+2 = 9 (approximately). Hence t_1 > t_2.

steps.title: Trace quicksort on {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
steps.formula_raw: Comparisons at each step = size of subarray - 1
steps.apply:
Step 1: pivot=1, compare with 2,3,4,5 => 4 comparisons; left=[], right={2,3,4,5}.
Step 2: pivot=2, compare with 3,4,5 => 3 comparisons; left=[], right={3,4,5}.
Step 3: pivot=3, compare with 4,5 => 2 comparisons.
Step 4: pivot=4, compare with 5 => 1 comparison.
Total t_1 = 4+3+2+1 = 10.
steps.note: Sorted input with first-element pivot always gives worst-case: O(n^2) total comparisons.

steps.title: Trace quicksort on {4, 1, 3, 2, 5}
steps.formula_raw: Comparisons at each step = size of subarray - 1
steps.apply:
Step 1: pivot=4, compare with 1,3,2,5 => 4 comparisons; left={1,3,2}, right={5}.
Step 2 (left subarray {1,3,2}): pivot=1, compare with 3,2 => 2 comparisons; left=[], right={3,2}.
Step 3 ({3,2}): pivot=3, compare with 2 => 1 comparison; left={2}, right=[].
Step 4 (right subarray {5}): size 1, no comparison.
Total t_2 = 4+2+1 = 7.
steps.note: A near-median pivot produces more balanced partitions, reducing total comparisons significantly.

steps.title: Compare t_1 and t_2
steps.note: The sorted input causes worst-case behavior; the partially unsorted input is handled more efficiently.

solution.result: t_1 > t_2, so the answer is B.

questions.formula_note: First-element pivot on an already-sorted array always creates maximally unbalanced partitions, yielding worst-case O(n^2) comparisons; a random or near-median pivot yields far fewer comparisons.

questions.question:
You have an array of n elements. Suppose you implement quicksort by always choosing the central element of the array as the pivot. Then the tightest upper bound for the worst case performance is
A. O(n^2)
B. O(n log n)
C. Theta(n log n)
D. Theta(n^2)
questions.to_find: The tightest upper bound on the worst case time complexity of quicksort when always choosing the central (middle-index) element as pivot.

understand.plain: Quicksort's worst case depends on how balanced the pivot choice is. Choosing the central element by index (middle position) does NOT guarantee a balanced partition in terms of values. An adversary can craft an input where the central element is always the minimum or maximum value, causing worst-case O(n^2) behaviour just like the naive first-element pivot. So the worst case is still O(n^2).

keywords.explain: Choosing the element at position n/2 (middle index) as pivot for each partition.
keywords.example: For array of 5 elements, always pick index 2 (0-based) as pivot.

keywords.explain: When every pivot selection produces maximally unbalanced partitions (0 elements on one side, n-1 on the other).
keywords.example: A specially crafted input can force the central element to always be the max/min, giving O(n^2) comparisons.

understand.visual_alt: Quicksort choosing central-index element as pivot can still degrade to O(n^2) if the input is adversarially constructed so the central element is always the extreme value.

given.aim: Find the tightest upper bound on worst-case time complexity of quicksort with central-element pivot.

terms.meaning: Number of elements in the array.
terms.connects: Determines the size of the recursion and number of comparisons.

terms.meaning: The element at the middle index (index n/2) of the current subarray.
terms.example: In {3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6}, central element is at index 4, which is 5.
terms.connects: Using middle index does NOT guarantee splitting by value, so worst case still possible.

terms.meaning: An adversarially constructed array where the central element is always the min or max value.
terms.example: Such inputs exist for any deterministic pivot-selection strategy.
terms.connects: Proves worst-case remains O(n^2) even with central-element pivot.

given.plan: Recall that any deterministic pivot selection (including central index) can be defeated by an adversarial input. The worst case recurrence becomes T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n), solving to O(n^2). Since O(n^2) is achievable and is the upper bound, the tightest bound is O(n^2).

steps.title: Understand what 'central element' means
steps.apply: Choosing the element at the middle index does NOT guarantee a median-value split. For example, in the array {1, 2, 3, ..., n} rearranged adversarially, the central-index element can always be the smallest remaining element.
steps.note: Do not confuse 'central index' with 'median value'. Only the median-of-medians algorithm guarantees balanced splits.

steps.title: Derive worst-case recurrence
steps.apply: When central-index element happens to be the minimum of the subarray at each step, the partition puts 0 elements on the left and n-1 on the right. This gives the recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + cn. Solving: T(n) = cn + c(n-1) + ... + c*1 = O(n^2).
steps.note: The worst case is the same as naive quicksort: O(n^2).

steps.title: Choose the tightest upper bound
steps.apply: Worst case is O(n^2). The question asks for an upper bound, not a tight bound (Theta). Option A is O(n^2), which is correct as the tightest upper bound. Option D (Theta(n^2)) would imply the worst case is always n^2, but average/best case is O(n log n), so Theta(n^2) is not correct for general worst-case. The tightest upper bound remains O(n^2).
steps.note: O(n^2) is the correct tightest upper bound for worst-case; Theta(n^2) would mean the worst case is always exactly n^2 growth, which is too strong a claim.

solution.result: The tightest upper bound for worst case performance is O(n^2), so the answer is A.

questions.formula_note: Deterministic pivot selection (including central-index) can always be defeated by adversarial input, giving worst-case recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) => O(n^2).

questions.question:
Which one of the following is the recurrence equation for the worst case time complexity of the quicksort algorithm for sorting n >= 2 numbers? In the recurrence equations given in the options below, c is a constant.
A. T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn
B. T(n) = T(n-1) + T(1) + cn
C. T(n) = 2T(n-2) + cn
D. T(n) = T(n/2) + cn
questions.to_find: The recurrence relation that correctly models the worst-case time complexity of quicksort.

understand.plain: In the worst case of quicksort, the pivot always ends up being the smallest or largest element. This means one partition has n-1 elements and the other has 0 elements (or 1 element including the pivot). This unbalanced split is captured by the recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + T(1) + cn, where T(n-1) is the larger subproblem, T(1) is the trivial one, and cn accounts for the partitioning work.

keywords.explain: Occurs when the pivot always picks the minimum or maximum element, causing maximally unbalanced partitions.
keywords.example: Sorting {1,2,3,4,5} with first-element pivot: 1 is always picked, giving partitions of size 0 and n-1.

keywords.explain: A mathematical equation expressing the cost of solving a problem of size n in terms of smaller subproblems.
keywords.example: T(n) = T(n-1) + cn means: solve a problem of size n-1 plus cn work at this level.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='200' viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='150' y='10' width='120' height='36' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='34' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T(n)</text><text x='210' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>partition cost: cn</text><line x1='130' y1='46' x2='80' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='290' y1='46' x2='340' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='30' y='90' width='100' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='114' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T(n-1)</text><rect x='290' y='90' width='100' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='340' y='114' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T(1)</text><text x='80' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>large part</text><text x='340' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>trivial part</text><text x='210' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Worst case: pivot is always min/max</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Recursion tree for worst-case quicksort: T(n) splits into T(n-1) (large partition) and T(1) (trivial partition of just the pivot), with cn work at each level for partitioning.

given.aim: Identify the correct recurrence for worst-case quicksort from the four given options.

terms.meaning: Time to sort n elements in worst case.
terms.example: T(5) is the time to sort 5 elements.
terms.connects: Expressed recursively in terms of smaller subproblem sizes.

terms.meaning: Time to sort the larger partition of n-1 elements after removing the pivot.
terms.example: After placing pivot at position 0, the right part has n-1 elements.
terms.connects: The dominant recursive term in worst-case quicksort.

terms.meaning: Cost of the partition step: scanning all n elements and placing them relative to pivot.
terms.example: Comparing each of n elements with the pivot costs proportional to n.
terms.connects: This is the non-recursive work done at each level of recursion.

given.plan: In worst case, pivot always lands at one extreme, giving partitions of size n-1 and 0 (or 1 if including pivot). The correct recurrence is T(n) = T(n-1) + T(1) + cn. Compare each option and eliminate: A is for merge sort (balanced split), C has no standard basis, D is for O(n log n) best case.

steps.title: Identify worst-case partition behavior
steps.apply: Worst case: pivot is always the smallest (or largest) element. After partitioning, left subarray has 0 elements, right subarray has n-1 elements (or vice versa). The pivot itself goes to its correct position (1 element). So subproblems are of size n-1 and 1 (or 0).
steps.note: This is the key insight: unbalanced partition => one subproblem of size n-1.

steps.title: Write the recurrence
steps.apply: T(n-1): time for the larger partition. T(1): time for the trivial partition (1 element). cn: cost of partitioning n elements. This matches option B.
steps.note: T(1) = c (constant), so this simplifies to T(n) = T(n-1) + cn, which gives T(n) = O(n^2).

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply:
A. T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn: This is merge sort (balanced split) — not worst-case quicksort.
C. T(n) = 2T(n-2) + cn: No standard algorithm has this pattern.
D. T(n) = T(n/2) + cn: This would be binary-search-like; not quicksort's worst case.
steps.note: Only option B correctly models the unbalanced split of worst-case quicksort.

solution.result: The correct recurrence is T(n) = T(n-1) + T(1) + cn, which is option B.

questions.formula_note: Worst-case quicksort recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + T(1) + cn arises when pivot is always the extreme element; this simplifies to T(n) = T(n-1) + cn => O(n^2).

questions.answer: A. (a, left_end, k) and (a + left_end + 1, n - left_end - 1, k - left_end - 1)
questions.to_find: The missing arguments in the recursive calls for the k-th smallest element function using quickselect.

understand.plain: The function partition() places all elements <= pivot on the left and all > pivot on the right, returning the pivot's final index (left_end). The k-th smallest search then has three cases: if the pivot is at position k (1-indexed: left_end+1 == k), it is the answer. If the pivot is to the right of position k, the k-th smallest is in the left part — recurse on the left subarray. If the pivot is to the left of position k, recurse on the right subarray but ask for the (k - left_end - 1)-th smallest in that subarray.

keywords.meaning: The 0-based index of the pivot after partitioning; the left part is a[0..left_end-1], pivot is at a[left_end], right part is a[left_end+1..n-1].
keywords.example: If left_end = 2, pivot is at index 2, left part has 2 elements, right part has n-3 elements.

keywords.explain: A selection algorithm based on quicksort's partition that finds the k-th smallest element in O(n) average time.
keywords.example: To find the 3rd smallest in {5,2,8,1,9}: partition, check if pivot is at index 2 (0-based), recurse if not.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='500' height='160' viewBox='0 0 500 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='40' width='160' height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a[0..left_end-1]</text><text x='90' y='83' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(left_end elements)</text><rect x='175' y='40' width='60' height='40' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='205' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>pivot</text><text x='205' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>a[left_end]</text><rect x='240' y='40' width='250' height='40' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='365' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a[left_end+1..n-1]</text><text x='365' y='83' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(n-left_end-1 elements)</text><text x='90' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>k < left_end+1: recurse left</text><text x='365' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>k > left_end+1: recurse right</text><text x='90' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>kth_smallest(a, left_end, k)</text><text x='365' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>kth_smallest(a+left_end+1, n-left_end-1, k-left_end-1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array partitioned by pivot: left part has elements <= pivot (indices 0 to left_end-1), pivot at left_end, right part at left_end+1 to n-1. If k-th smallest is in left, recurse left with same k; if in right, recurse right with adjusted rank k-left_end-1.

given.aim: Fill in the missing recursive call arguments for quickselect-based k-th smallest finder.

terms.meaning: 0-based index of pivot after partition; there are left_end elements to its left.
terms.example: left_end = 3 means pivot is at index 3; elements at 0,1,2 are smaller-or-equal, elements at 4..n-1 are larger.
terms.connects: Determines which subarray to recurse into and how to adjust k.

terms.meaning: 1-based rank of the pivot after partitioning (since indices are 0-based but rank is 1-based).
terms.example: If left_end = 2, the pivot is the 3rd smallest found so far (rank 3).
terms.connects: Compared against k to decide: found, go left, or go right.

terms.meaning: Adjusted rank when recursing into the right subarray: we skip over left_end+1 elements (left part + pivot).
terms.example: If k=5 and left_end=2, we want rank 5-2-1=2 in the right subarray.
terms.connects: Necessary adjustment so the recursive call finds the correct element within the right subarray.

given.plan: Case 1: left_end + 1 > k means k-th smallest is in the left part (a[0..left_end-1], size = left_end), so recurse kth_smallest(a, left_end, k). Case 2: left_end + 1 < k means k-th smallest is in the right part (a[left_end+1..n-1], size = n-left_end-1), and we want rank k - (left_end+1) = k-left_end-1 in that subarray, so recurse kth_smallest(a + left_end + 1, n - left_end - 1, k - left_end - 1).

steps.title: Analyze the case left_end + 1 > k (k-th smallest is in left part)
steps.apply: The left subarray is a[0..left_end-1], which has left_end elements. We want the k-th smallest among these left_end elements. The subarray starts at a (same pointer), has size left_end, and we still want rank k. So the first missing argument is (a, left_end, k).
steps.note: No rank adjustment needed because we are looking at the same leftmost portion of the array; k is still valid.

steps.title: Analyze the case left_end + 1 < k (k-th smallest is in right part)
steps.formula_raw: kth_smallest(a + left_end + 1, n - left_end - 1, k - left_end - 1)
steps.apply: The right subarray starts at a[left_end+1], so the pointer is a + left_end + 1. Its size is n - left_end - 1. Within this subarray, we want the rank (k - left_end - 1)-th smallest, because k minus the (left_end + 1) elements already to the left (including pivot) gives the relative rank. So the second missing argument is (a + left_end + 1, n - left_end - 1, k - left_end - 1).
steps.note: The rank must be adjusted: we skip over left_end elements in the left part and 1 pivot element, so subtract left_end + 1 from k.

steps.title: Match with the options
steps.apply:
First argument: (a, left_end, k) -- matches option A.
Second argument: (a + left_end + 1, n - left_end - 1, k - left_end - 1) -- matches option A.
Option B uses wrong 3rd arg (n-left_end-1 instead of k-left_end-1) for second call.
Options C and D have the two recursive calls in wrong order (right part first, left part second).
steps.note: The code checks left_end+1 > k first (k in left), so the first kth_smallest call must be for the left part.

solution.result: The missing arguments are (a, left_end, k) and (a + left_end + 1, n - left_end - 1, k - left_end - 1), so the answer is A.

questions.formula_note: Quickselect (selection via partition): recurse into the left subarray with same k if pivot rank > k, or into right subarray with adjusted rank k - left_end - 1 if pivot rank < k.
